text: In thymectomy, less invasive surgical options have arisen as alternatives to median synotomy, but thymectomy or robotic thymectomy through the unilateral or bilateral approach. However, the lateral approach causes intercostal nerve injury. We reported a new surgical procedure Single-port thymectomy, in which the port and devices used in single-port surgery are employed for thymectomy through an infrasoundary approach.

The surgical setup for a single-port thymectomy is displayed, showing the specialized port and instruments used, including grasping forceps and a vessel sealing device. The patient is positioned supine with legs spread, while the surgeon stands between the legs and the assistant on the right side. The operating area is arranged at the cranial side of the bed.

A three-centimeter transverse incision is made one centimeter below the xiphoid process. The surgeon uses a finger to blindly dissect the back of the sternum, followed by the insertion of a single-port surgery port through the subxiphoid incision. CO₂ insufflation at eight millimeters Hg displaces the lungs and pericardium, creating space behind the sternum. The vessel sealing device is used to detach the thymus from the sternum. The mediastinum is dissected to open the chest cavity bilaterally, and the thymus, thymic tumor, and surrounding fatty tissue anterior to the phrenic nerves are dissected from the pericardium.
The surgeon isolates the brachiocephalic vein and separates the right upper pole of the thymus. The thymic vein is cut using a vessel sealing device.
The surgeon places the resected thymus into a bag within the mediastinum and removes it through the sub-xiphoid incision. A small French drain is inserted through the same incision.

The camera scope is inserted through the subxiphoid incision, providing a clear view of the neck area and phrenic nerves. The operative field resembles that of a median sternotomy, allowing for detailed visualization without the need for sternotomy or risk of intercostal nerve injury.
